macro_segments.title: The Status of a Personal Fast: Eating the Night Before and Saying Aneinu

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's ruling: An individual who accepts a fast can eat the entire preceding night and still recite the fast prayer the next morning
Contrast case: One who fasted the entire previous night but did not formally accept the fast—cannot recite Aneinu despite fasting for twenty-four hours
Abaye's resolution: A half-day fast can be valid and worthy of Aneinu if formally accepted; the problem in the contrast case is lack of formal acceptance (kabbalah) from the day before
Discussion of when and how Aneinu is recited: the Bahag, Tosafot, and Sephardic practice regarding recitation at Shacharit versus Mincha

macro_segments.title: Duration and Completion: The Requirement to Fast Until Sunset

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda: Any fast that does not end with sunset does not count as a valid fast
Contradiction from historical sources: The men of the mishmar (priestly watch) would fast but not complete the day
Rabbi Elazar bar Rabbi Tzadok: The holiday of his family (tenth of Av) overrode Tisha B'Av; they fasted but did not complete it because it was their Yom Tov
Rabbi Yochanan's example: 'I will be in a fast until I get to my house'—a fast without completing the day, explained as motivated by avoiding eating at someone else's house

macro_segments.title: Formal Acceptance (Kabbalah) from Afternoon: The Foundation of a Valid Fast

micro_segments.title: Shmuel: Any fast that lacks formal acceptance from before sunset (mibe'od yom) does not count as a fast
Rav Yosef's question: What happens if one fasts the entire day without prior acceptance? Rabbah bar Shila's answer: like a bellows full of air—worthless
When to accept a fast: Rava says at Mincha time; Shmuel says during the Mincha prayer itself (Shmoneh Esrei)
Tosafot and the integration of personal fast acceptance into the formal davening, particularly into Shomea Tefillah
The conceptual link between ta'anit and tefillah: Fasting is not merely abstinence but a formal religious status dependent on prayer

macro_segments.title: Megillat Ta'anit and the Mechanism of Personal Vows (Nedarim)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef cites Megillat Ta'anit regarding individuals who accept fasts that conflict with designated holidays
Textual analysis: The dispute between Rabbi Chiyya and Rabbi Shimon ben Rabbi over whether Megillat Ta'anit includes the phrase 'in prayer' (betzalo)
Scholion of Megillat Ta'anit: An individual accepting Mondays and Thursdays year-round—whose vow takes precedence when holidays are declared, and the principle of being grandfathered in
The concept of making up (mashlim) a fast: Fasting is not merely a practice but a countable unit that can be deferred to another day
Reifying the fast: The fast is treated as an object with status (like a Nazir's status) rather than merely a behavioral practice

macro_segments.title: The Mechanics of Eating and the Onset of the Fast Day

micro_segments.title: Until when may one eat before a fast begins: Rabbi holds until amud hashachar; Rabbi Elazar bar Rabbi Shimon holds until cock crow
Abaye's refinement: One may eat only if one has not finished (gamar) the meal; once the meal is concluded, the fast conceptually begins
Disputes: Can one continue eating after rising from the table, after sleep, or only while actually in the middle of eating?
Rav Ashi on dozing (misnamnem): A liminal state between sleep and wakefulness that does not yet seal the onset of the fast
Practical application: Intent and preparation matter—if one sets an alarm intending to wake and eat, one may do so; otherwise, unplanned waking before amud hashachar does not permit eating

macro_segments.title: Confusion Between Personal and Communal Fasts: The Shoe Issue and Rav Sheshet's Challenge

micro_segments.title: Rav Chana: An individual accepting a personal fast should still abstain from wearing shoes, lest there be confusion about whether he intended a full communal fast
The reification of fast types: Ta'anit Tzibur is not merely a description but an object—one can accept 'a communal fast' as a yachid, taking on its stringencies
Rav Sheshet's critique: He observes rabbinic students putting on shoes at a 'beit ta'anita' (house of fasting) and challenges them—are you also eating?
Variations in practice: Abaye and Rava wore a hapantza (slipper); Mar Zutra switched shoes from foot to foot as a compromise (shinuy)
Shmuel's ruling and Babylonian practice: No ta'anit tzibur in Babylon except Tisha B'Av; therefore communal fasts in Babylon never reach the stringency of the night-before type, hence shoes are worn
The yachid-tzibur tension: When individuals know the halakhic leniency but the community observes stringently, the appearance of separation creates a public perception problem

topical_tags.term: Yachid vs. Tzibur (individual vs. communal fast)
Seudah Mafseket (the meal that ends eating)
